item.title: Bhikkhus, this is the ultimate livelihood among livelihoods, namely, going for alms. In the world, the abusive term is: 'You are a beggar, carrying a…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, this is the ultimate livelihood among livelihoods, namely, going for alms. In the world, the abusive term is: 'You are a beggar, carrying a bowl, wandering everywhere.' Yet this livelihood is accepted by good clansmen who live for the sake of the goal, who live dependent on the goal, not driven to it by fear of kings, fear of thieves, by debt, by fear, not because of loss of livelihood, but thinking: 'I am fallen into birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair, fallen into suffering, overcome by suffering. Perhaps the complete destruction of this whole mass of suffering can be made known.' Bhikkhus, such a clansman who has gone forth, if he still has craving for sensual pleasures, strong greed, a hateful mind, evil thoughts, forgetfulness, lack of clear comprehension, unconcentrated, with distracted mind and loose faculties — then, bhikkhus, like a funeral torch taken from a cremation ground, burning at both ends, smeared with excrement in the middle, not serving as a log in the village or in the forest — I say that such a person is like that simile. Though he has left household wealth, he does not fulfill the goal of asceticism.

From: Itivuttaka 91
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Therefore, Cunda, the robe that I have allowed you to wear is sufficient for you to ward off cold, to ward off heat, to ward…
item.description:
Therefore, Cunda, the robe that I have allowed you to wear is sufficient for you to ward off cold, to ward off heat, to ward off the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and creeping things, and to conceal your private parts.
The almsfood that I have allowed you to use is sufficient to nourish the body, to sustain the body, to avoid harm, and to support the holy life, with the hope: 'Thus I shall destroy old feelings and not arouse new feelings. So my life will be blameless and I shall dwell at ease.'

From: Dīgha Nikāya 29 – Pāsādikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, in the future there will be bhikkhus who live in association with gardeners and with novice monks. When living in association with gardeners and…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, in the future there will be bhikkhus who live in association with gardeners and with novice monks. When living in association with gardeners and novice monks, bhikkhus, it is to be expected that they will live enjoying stored-up goods, and they will make rough signs regarding land and agricultural products. This, bhikkhus, is the fifth fear regarding the future, not yet arisen now but will arise in the future. You should fully understand them. Having fully understood them, you should strive to abandon that fear.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.80
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Just as, bhikkhus, a long-haired she-goat enters a thorny thicket; here and there it gets caught, here and there it gets entangled, here and there…
item.description:
Just as, bhikkhus, a long-haired she-goat enters a thorny thicket; here and there it gets caught, here and there it gets entangled, here and there it gets bound, here and there it falls into misfortune and calamity. So too, bhikkhus, a certain bhikkhu, overcome by gains, honor, and fame, his mind obsessed, in the morning puts on his robe, takes his bowl, and enters the village or town for alms. Here and there, he gets caught, here and there he gets entangled, here and there he gets bound, here and there he falls into misfortune and calamity.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 17.4
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: What do you think, Māgandiya? Here, a person was formerly infatuated with forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with…
item.description:
What do you think, Māgandiya? Here, a person was formerly infatuated with forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensual desire, enticing. Later, having truly understood the origination, the passing away, the gratification, the danger, and the escape of forms, having abandoned craving for forms, having removed fever for forms, he dwells with craving cut off, with inner peace. Surely, Māgandiya, that person would not long for those former forms, would he?

From: Majjhima Nikāya 75 – Māgaṇḍiyasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Dhammasava: Having reflected, I went forth homeless. I have attained the three knowledges; done is the Buddha's teaching.
item.description:
Elder Dhammasava:

Having reflected, I went forth homeless.
I have attained the three knowledges; done is the Buddha's teaching.

From: Theragāthā 1.107
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: I do not see any other sound, bhikkhus, that so obsesses and takes hold of a man's mind as the sound of a woman. The…
item.description:
I do not see any other sound, bhikkhus, that so obsesses and takes hold of a man's mind as the sound of a woman. The sound of a woman, bhikkhus, obsesses and takes hold of a man's mind.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.2
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, what is Right Concentration? Bhikkhus, here a bhikkhu, secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and abides in the first jhāna, which…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, what is Right Concentration? Bhikkhus, here a bhikkhu, secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome states, enters and abides in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by initial and sustained thought, with rapture and happiness born of seclusion. With the stilling of initial and sustained thought, he enters and abides in the second jhāna, which has internal clarity and unification of mind, without initial and sustained thought, with rapture and happiness born of concentration. With the fading away of rapture, he dwells equanimous, mindful and clearly comprehending, and experiences happiness with the body; he enters and abides in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare: 'Equanimous and mindful, one dwells happily.' With the abandoning of pleasure and pain, and with the previous passing away of joy and grief, he enters and abides in the fourth jhāna, which is neither painful nor pleasant, with purity of mindfulness due to equanimity. Bhikkhus, this is called Right Concentration.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 22 – Mahāsatipaṭṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Those who have cut off all fetters, Subdued, liberated, unshaken, without longing — At the proper time offer them the sacrifice. The brahmin seeking merit,…
item.description:
Those who have cut off all fetters,
Subdued, liberated, unshaken, without longing —
At the proper time offer them the sacrifice.
The brahmin seeking merit, give thus.

Those who have destroyed greed, hatred, and delusion,
With taints destroyed, the holy life fulfilled —
At the proper time offer them the sacrifice.
The brahmin seeking merit, give thus.

From: Sutta Nipāta 3.5
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Just as, bhikkhus, a dung-beetle, feeding on dung, full of dung, stuffed with dung, sees a great pile of dung before it. It despises other…
item.description:
Just as, bhikkhus, a dung-beetle, feeding on dung, full of dung, stuffed with dung, sees a great pile of dung before it. It despises other dung-beetles and says: 'I feed on dung, I am full of dung, I am stuffed with dung, and before me is this great pile of dung.'

So too, bhikkhus, a certain bhikkhu, overcome by gains, honor, and fame, his mind obsessed, in the morning puts on his robe, takes his bowl, and enters the village or town for alms. There he eats and drinks to satiety, and is invited again for the next day, and his bowl is filled.

He goes to the monastery and boasts among the Saṅgha of bhikkhus: 'I have eaten and drunk to satiety, I am invited again for tomorrow, and my bowl is full. I receive requisites such as robes, almsfood, lodging, and medicines. But these other bhikkhus, of little merit and influence, do not receive requisites such as robes, almsfood, lodging, and medicines.'

Overcome by gains, honor, and fame, his mind obsessed, he despises other virtuous bhikkhus. This will bring that fool long-term harm and suffering.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 17.5
📖 Read this sutta
